sections.plain_summary: Hydrogen SIBO is a bacterial overgrowth in the small intestine that produces excess hydrogen gas, causing bloating, pain, and changes in bowel habits. It develops when bacteria that normally live in the colon migrate to and overgrow in the small intestine, disrupting digestion and nutrient absorption.
sections.detailed_summary: Small Intestinal Bacterial Overgrowth (SIBO) occurs when bacteria in the small intestine exceed normal levels (>10⁵ CFU/mL), fermenting carbohydrates and producing hydrogen gas. Hydrogen SIBO specifically refers to the hydrogen-predominant form, as opposed to methane-producing or hydrogen sulfide-producing variants. The condition disrupts normal digestion, causes inflammation, and impairs nutrient absorption. Risk factors include reduced stomach acid, impaired motility, anatomical changes, and previous food poisoning. Hydrogen SIBO typically causes acute, fluctuating symptoms and may be more common in people with IBS, especially the diarrhea-predominant type.

prevalence.summary: Estimated to affect 5–15% of people with IBS symptoms, though estimates vary widely depending on testing method and population studied.
prevalence.estimate: 5–15% of people with IBS; roughly 1–3% of the general asymptomatic population
prevalence.notes: Prevalence estimates are highly contested because the diagnostic test (lactulose breath test) lacks standardization; different labs use different cutoff thresholds. SIBO is overdiagnosed in functional medicine circles and underrecognized in mainstream gastroenterology. Geographic variation, age, and baseline IBS prevalence affect estimates.

subgroups.name: Hydrogen SIBO with diarrhea
subgroups.description: Presents with loose stools or diarrhea; hydrogen gas accelerates transit time through the small intestine, reducing water reabsorption.
distinguishing_features: Loose or watery stools, often worse after meals
More acute symptom onset after eating

subgroups.name: Hydrogen SIBO with constipation
subgroups.description: Presents with constipation despite bacterial overgrowth; may occur if underlying motility disorder dominates, or if hydrogen triggers visceral hypersensitivity rather than transit acceleration.
distinguishing_features: Often associated with slower small-bowel transit

subgroups.name: Post-infectious hydrogen SIBO
subgroups.description: Develops after acute gastroenteritis; bacteria persist and overgrow following recovery from acute infection.
distinguishing_features: Clear temporal link to food poisoning or traveler's diarrhea
Symptoms begin days to weeks after acute illness
May have history of prolonged antibiotic use
Often associated with persistent low-grade inflammation

symptoms.notes: Often worsens throughout the day or after meals; hydrogen gas accumulates in the small intestine, causing visible or felt distension. May improve or worsen with bowel movements.
Can be sharp, dull, or crampy; typically postprandial (after eating). Often improves temporarily after a bowel movement.
Hydrogen gas speeds up small-bowel transit, reducing water reabsorption. Urgency and frequency often increase after meals, especially carbohydrate-rich ones.
Less common in hydrogen SIBO but may occur if underlying dysmotility dominates or if symptoms trigger reduced oral intake.
Hydrogen is expelled through the rectum; may be accompanied by belching if gas refluxes into the stomach.
Often postprandial; may be related to bloating, increased gastric pressure, or bacterial metabolites.
Feeling full quickly despite eating small amounts; related to bloating and visceral hypersensitivity.
May relate to malabsorption, bacterial byproducts, or systemic inflammation; timing often correlates with meals.
Often due to malabsorption of vitamins (B12, iron, fat-soluble vitamins) or chronic low-grade inflammation.
Anecdotally reported; may relate to systemic inflammation or malabsorption of anti-inflammatory nutrients.
Some patients report headaches triggered by meals; may relate to hydrogen or bacterial metabolites, though evidence is limited.
Uncommon but reported; may relate to food sensitivities exacerbated by malabsorption or increased intestinal permeability.

key_patterns: Bloating that worsens during the day or after meals, especially carbohydrates or fermented foods
Abdominal pain or cramping that improves or worsens with bowel movements
Diarrhea or loose stools without an obvious infectious cause, lasting weeks or months
Symptoms that fluctuate based on diet, with worse flares after sugar, grains, or high-FODMAP foods
History of food poisoning or acute gastroenteritis preceding symptom onset
Bloating and gas despite eating small portions or a restrictive diet
Fatigue, brain fog, or nutrient deficiency symptoms alongside GI complaints

at_home.name: Symptom and food diary
at_home.description: Record the time and content of each meal, and note bloating, pain, flatulence, and stool type within the next 2–4 hours. Look for consistent patterns linking specific foods to symptoms. Use a 1–10 severity scale for symptoms.
at_home.safety_notes: Low-risk. Maintain normal nutrition; do not restrict foods excessively based on diary observations alone. Diaries are suggestive, not diagnostic.

at_home.name: Low-FODMAP elimination trial
at_home.description: Strictly follow a low-FODMAP diet for 5–7 days, avoiding onions, garlic, wheat, certain fruits, and legumes. Document bloating, pain, and stool changes daily. If symptoms improve >50%, reintroduce foods one at a time to identify triggers.
at_home.safety_notes: Safe for short-term use but can lead to nutritional gaps if prolonged. Not a diagnosis; response supports fermentation-based mechanisms but does not prove SIBO. Stop if symptoms worsen or if you feel unwell.

clinical.name: Lactulose breath test (LBT)
clinical.description: Patient fasts 12 hours, then ingests 10–25 g lactulose and exhales into collection tubes at timed intervals (usually 0, 15, 30, 60, 90, 120 minutes). Hydrogen and methane in breath are measured. SIBO is typically defined as hydrogen >20 ppm or methane >10 ppm at baseline or within 90 minutes of ingestion. Hydrogen SIBO shows elevated hydrogen with normal or low methane.
clinical.availability: Widely available through gastroenterology clinics, functional medicine practitioners, and some primary care practices. Requires fasting and 2–3 hours of clinic time. Results available within 1 week.

clinical.name: Glucose hydrogen breath test (GBT)
clinical.description: Similar to LBT but uses 25–50 g glucose instead of lactulose. Some argue glucose is more specific to SIBO in the small intestine (lactulose may ferment in the colon even without SIBO). Hydrogen SIBO shows elevated hydrogen. Results interpreted the same way as LBT.
clinical.availability: Less commonly available than LBT but offered by some gastroenterology and functional medicine practices. May require special ordering.

clinical.name: Small intestinal aspirate culture
clinical.description: Gold-standard diagnostic test but rarely performed due to invasiveness. Endoscopy is used to aspirate fluid directly from the small intestine (usually at the proximal jejunum), which is cultured. SIBO is defined as ≥10⁵ CFU/mL. Directly identifies bacterial species and allows antibiotic sensitivity testing. Hydrogen SIBO shows predominance of hydrogen-producing organisms.
clinical.availability: Only available at specialized centers or major academic medical centers. Requires sedated endoscopy. Often reserved for cases where breath testing is inconclusive or treatment failure is suspected.

clinical.name: Stool organic acids test (OAT)
clinical.description: Measures organic acids in stool that reflect bacterial metabolism. Elevated markers like d-lactic acid, short-chain fatty acids, or fungal metabolites are interpreted as suggestive of dysbiosis or SIBO. Not standardized and lacks strong clinical validation for SIBO diagnosis specifically.
clinical.availability: Available through functional medicine labs and some alternative practitioners. Relatively inexpensive but interpretation varies widely.

clinical.name: Comprehensive stool analysis
clinical.description: Analyzes bacterial composition, diversity, inflammation markers, and digestive function. May reveal dysbiosis or reduced beneficial bacteria, supporting SIBO suspicion, but cannot definitively diagnose SIBO.
clinical.availability: Available through functional medicine and naturopathic practitioners. Increasing availability through mainstream labs. Cost varies.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: SIBO is found in 5–15% of people with IBS-D. IBS and SIBO share overlapping symptoms (bloating, pain, diarrhea) but IBS is a functional disorder without bacterial overgrowth. Some researchers argue SIBO is a biological cause of IBS in a subset of patients.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal motility disorder (dysmotility)
related_conditions.explanation: Reduced or uncoordinated small-bowel contractions allow bacteria to overgrow. Conditions like scleroderma, diabetes, and narcotic use impair motility and increase SIBO risk. Hydrogen SIBO may be secondary to an underlying motility defect.

related_conditions.name: Gastric ulcer disease or low stomach acid (achlorhydria)
related_conditions.explanation: Stomach acid normally kills ingested bacteria. Reduced acid (from PPI use, aging, or atrophic gastritis) allows bacteria to survive transit and colonize the small intestine, increasing SIBO risk.

related_conditions.name: Post-infectious irritable bowel syndrome (PI-IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: SIBO is a proposed biological mechanism for PI-IBS; infection-triggered changes in intestinal motility, immune function, or barrier integrity allow bacterial overgrowth and persistent symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Celiac disease and non-celiac gluten sensitivity
related_conditions.explanation: Both conditions impair small-intestinal barrier function and are associated with increased SIBO prevalence. Gluten exposure may worsen both conditions simultaneously.

related_conditions.name: Crohn's disease
related_conditions.explanation: Crohn's disease increases SIBO risk through reduced stomach acid, altered motility, and strictures. SIBO may worsen inflammation and symptom control in Crohn's patients.

related_conditions.name: Pancreatic insufficiency
related_conditions.explanation: Reduced pancreatic enzymes impair carbohydrate and fat digestion, allowing undigested substrates to feed bacteria and worsen fermentation. May occur together or as a consequence of each other.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: Some fibromyalgia patients have SIBO; shared features include visceral hypersensitivity, abnormal immune activation, and altered pain processing. Causality remains unclear.

related_conditions.name: Leaky gut syndrome (increased intestinal permeability)
related_conditions.explanation: Hydrogen and bacterial metabolites produced in SIBO may increase intestinal permeability, and vice versa. The two conditions may reinforce each other.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Some SIBO-associated bacteria produce histamine; dietary histamine sensitivity may worsen in the context of SIBO and improve with SIBO treatment.

strategies.name: Low-FODMAP diet
strategies.description: Restrict fermentable carbohydrates (onions, garlic, wheat, high-fructose fruits, legumes, sugar alcohols). These starve bacteria of substrate. Typically done in two phases: strict elimination for 4–6 weeks, then stepwise reintroduction to identify tolerance thresholds. Many patients see 50–70% symptom improvement.
strategies.notes: Most effective during the acute phase. Long-term, strict low-FODMAP diets may reduce beneficial fiber and lead to nutritional gaps. Work with a dietitian to ensure balanced nutrition. Reintroduction is important to avoid unnecessary restriction.

strategies.name: Specific carbohydrate diet (SCD)
strategies.description: Eliminates disaccharides and polysaccharides, allowing only monosaccharides (fruit, honey, well-cooked vegetables). Theory is that only simple sugars are absorbed efficiently, leaving less substrate for bacteria. Anecdotally effective for some SIBO patients, especially those with concurrent inflammatory bowel disease.
strategies.notes: More restrictive than low-FODMAP; longer learning curve. May be difficult to sustain long-term. Evidence is primarily from patient testimonials, not RCTs.

strategies.name: Elemental diet
strategies.description: Replace solid food with a liquid formula containing amino acids, simple sugars, and fats in pre-digested form, requiring minimal bacterial fermentation. Typically done for 2–4 weeks. Some studies show 80% symptom improvement and bacterial load reduction.
strategies.notes: Effective but requires discipline and causes temporary social disruption. May lead to temporary weight loss. Cost varies ($100–$300/week). Reintroduction of solid food should be gradual and guided by a clinician.

strategies.name: Rifaxomicin antibiotic
strategies.description: A non-absorbed antibiotic effective against many gram-negative bacteria in the small intestine. Typically given at 550 mg three times daily for 14 days. Studies show 30–50% symptomatic improvement and bacterial eradication in 40–60% of cases. Often used as first-line treatment in gastroenterology.
strategies.notes: Expensive (cost: $2000–$4000 for a 14-day course in the US) and not always covered by insurance. Risk of relapse is high (20–30% within 6 months) if underlying motility or other predisposing factors are not addressed. May cause mild GI upset or headache.

strategies.name: Neomycin antibiotic
strategies.description: An older, non-absorbed aminoglycoside antibiotic. Dose: 500 mg three to four times daily for 7–14 days. Less expensive than rifaxomicin but efficacy is lower (25–40%). Sometimes used off-label or in combination with other antibiotics.
strategies.notes: Some systemic absorption occurs; caution in elderly patients or those with renal impairment. May cause diarrhea or ototoxicity with prolonged use. Less commonly recommended now due to availability of rifaxomicin.

strategies.name: Herbal antimicrobials
strategies.description: Includes oregano oil, thyme, berberine, allicin (garlic), and other plant-based compounds with antimicrobial properties. Often used in combination ('herbal protocols'). Course typically 4–12 weeks. Efficacy is difficult to measure but patient-reported improvement rates are 20–50%.
strategies.notes: Less rigorously studied than antibiotics. May be better tolerated and support normal microbiota better, but response is slower and more variable. Cost is low to moderate. Some herbals can interact with medications or cause side effects (e.g., oregano oil may irritate the gut).

strategies.name: Prokinetic agents (metoclopramide, domperidone)
strategies.description: Enhance small-intestine muscle contractions to improve clearance of bacteria. Metoclopramide (10 mg before meals) or domperidone (10–20 mg before meals) used for 4–12 weeks. May prevent relapse if underlying dysmotility exists.
strategies.notes: Metoclopramide carries a black-box warning for tardive dyskinesia with long-term use (>3 months); domperidone is not available in the US. More effective for secondary SIBO (from known motility disorder) than primary SIBO.

strategies.name: Probiotics
strategies.description: Introduce beneficial bacteria (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium, Saccharomyces boulardii) to restore normal microbiota. Typical dose: 10–50 billion CFU daily for 8–12 weeks. Mixed evidence; some patients benefit, others worsen.
strategies.notes: Efficacy is strain-dependent and highly individual. Some SIBO patients report worsening bloating on probiotics. Use after antibiotic treatment may help restore flora. Quality varies widely; choose tested, multi-strain formulas.

strategies.name: Prebiotics (inulin, FOS, resistant starch)
strategies.description: Feed beneficial bacteria to support their growth and short-chain fatty acid production. Typically started slowly (small amounts of resistant starch or inulin) and increased gradually over weeks.
strategies.notes: Must be introduced carefully in SIBO patients; too much too fast can worsen bloating by feeding pathogenic bacteria. Better used after successful treatment to rebuild healthy flora. Not recommended during active SIBO.

strategies.name: Digestive enzymes
strategies.description: Proteases, lipases, and amylases help break down food, reducing undigested substrates available to bacteria. Taken with meals. Limited evidence specifically for SIBO.
strategies.notes: May help if concurrent pancreatic insufficiency exists. Generally safe but expensive. Efficacy is low if malabsorption is driven primarily by bacterial overgrowth rather than enzyme deficiency.

strategies.name: Acid suppression (PPI or H2 blockers)
strategies.description: While stomach acid reduction is a risk factor for SIBO, short-term use of a PPI (e.g., omeprazole 20 mg daily) for 4–8 weeks may reduce acid damage during acute flares. However, long-term use worsens SIBO risk.
strategies.notes: Use cautiously and only if there is active reflux or gastritis. Discontinue as soon as symptoms resolve. Not recommended for SIBO prevention or maintenance. Consider alternative approaches (dietary modification, stress reduction) first.

strategies.name: Stress reduction and sleep optimization
strategies.description: Chronic stress impairs intestinal motility, reduces stomach acid, and weakens immune function. Meditation, yoga, CBT, or other relaxation practices; aim for 7–9 hours of sleep nightly. May take 4–8 weeks to see GI benefits.
strategies.notes: Addresses upstream factors and improves overall SIBO management. Works synergistically with diet and treatment. No direct antimicrobial effect but can reduce symptom severity and speed recovery.

strategies.name: Meal timing and eating slowly
strategies.description: Eat three meals (not frequent snacking) spaced 4–5 hours apart to allow the migrating motor complex (MMC) to clear the small intestine between meals. Chew thoroughly and eat slowly (20–30 min per meal) to reduce bacterial load of ingested food and improve digestion.
strategies.notes: Simple, no-cost intervention. MMC activity is reduced during eating and fasting periods; spacing meals optimizes bacterial clearance. Snacking disrupts this cycle and feeds bacteria.

strategies.name: Cooked vs. raw vegetables
strategies.description: Eating cooked vegetables instead of raw may be easier to digest and less likely to ferment, especially during acute phases. Raw vegetables have higher fiber and FODMAP content.
strategies.notes: Individual tolerance varies. Some patients benefit from cooked vegetables; others do not see a difference. Not a rigid rule.

strategies.name: Adequate hydration
strategies.description: Drink 8–10 glasses of water daily, especially if diarrhea is present. Proper hydration supports intestinal motility and prevents electrolyte depletion.
strategies.notes: Simple and beneficial for overall health. Do not overhydrate (>4 liters daily), which may worsen bloating or electrolyte imbalance.

strategies.name: Exercise and physical activity
strategies.description: 30 minutes of moderate activity (walking, yoga, swimming) most days supports intestinal motility and reduces stress. Avoid intense exercise during acute symptom flares.
strategies.notes: Gentle exercise is often better tolerated than intense workouts. May take 2–4 weeks to see benefits. Listen to your body; if exercise worsens symptoms, scale back intensity or duration.

strategies.name: Magnesium supplementation
strategies.description: Magnesium supports muscle relaxation and intestinal motility. Dose: 200–400 mg daily (glycinate or threonate forms are better tolerated). Take in the evening to avoid laxative effect.
strategies.notes: Especially useful if constipation or muscle tension accompanies SIBO. May cause loose stools if dose is too high. Magnesium malate or citrate can worsen bloating in some patients due to malate/citrate fermentation.

strategies.name: Pharmaceutical motility agents (Prucalopride)
strategies.description: A 5-HT4 agonist that enhances intestinal contractions. Dose: 1–2 mg daily. Used off-label for SIBO-associated constipation or dysmotility. Studies in constipation show benefit; limited SIBO-specific data.
strategies.notes: Not as well-studied as metoclopramide for SIBO. Requires gastroenterology or specialized care for prescribing. May be covered by insurance for constipation-predominant SIBO.

things_to_avoid: High-FODMAP foods (onions, garlic, wheat, honey, high-fructose fruits, legumes, sugar alcohols) — these ferment and feed bacteria, worsening bloating and gas.
Sugar and refined carbohydrates — they are rapidly fermented by bacteria, producing gas and acid.
Alcohol and caffeine — both can impair motility and irritate the gut, worsening symptoms.
Carbonated beverages — these introduce gas directly into the GI tract, exacerbating bloating.
Large meals — they overwhelm the small intestine and feed bacteria; eat smaller, frequent balanced meals instead.
Fatty foods in excess — while some fat is necessary, high-fat meals slow motility and can worsen bloating in some patients.
Raw vegetables and fibrous salads during acute flares — cook vegetables to reduce fermentation substrate.
Artificial sweeteners (sorbitol, mannitol, xylitol) — many are poorly absorbed and ferment, worsening gas.
Processed foods with additives and emulsifiers — may damage the intestinal barrier and promote dysbiosis.
Unnecessary long-term PPI or H2 blocker use — reduces stomach acid, increasing SIBO risk.
Antibiotics (unless prescribed for SIBO) — they kill beneficial bacteria and increase SIBO relapse risk if not combined with dietary and motility support.
Excessive stress without mitigation — stress impairs motility and immune function, exacerbating SIBO.
Prolonged fasting or skipped meals — the migrating motor complex requires regular meal spacing; skipping meals or extended fasting can disrupt clearance cycles.
Over-the-counter laxatives (except as directed for constipation-SIBO) — they may alter motility patterns unfavorably or cause dependency.

key_levers.lever: Fix underlying motility or gastric acid dysfunction
key_levers.why_it_matters: If reduced stomach acid or impaired small-intestine contractions are the root cause, treating SIBO without addressing these allows relapse. Addressing the upstream cause dramatically improves long-term remission.
key_levers.how_to_start: Work with a gastroenterologist to assess motility (scintigraphy or manometry if available) and stomach acid (consider stopping PPIs gradually if not medically necessary). Check for scleroderma, diabetes, or other systemic causes.
key_levers.timeline: 6–12 weeks to see improvement; assess at 3 months.

key_levers.lever: Adopt a sustainable, personalized low-fermentation diet
key_levers.why_it_matters: Diet is the foundation of long-term SIBO management. Restrictive diets that cannot be sustained lead to relapse once normal eating resumes. Finding the least restrictive diet that keeps symptoms manageable is key.
key_levers.how_to_start: Work with a dietitian experienced in SIBO to identify your personal FODMAP threshold. Start with a low-FODMAP elimination phase (4–6 weeks), then systematically reintroduce foods one at a time to expand choices. Use a symptom log to track what you tolerate.
key_levers.timeline: 8–12 weeks to identify personal tolerance patterns.

key_levers.lever: Optimize meal timing and eating habits
key_levers.why_it_matters: The migrating motor complex (MMC) clears bacteria between meals. Eating three regular meals spaced 4–5 hours apart, without snacking, optimizes this clearance cycle. This simple change can reduce bacterial load and prevent relapse.
key_levers.how_to_start: Stop snacking. Eat breakfast, lunch, and dinner at consistent times (e.g., 7 am, 12:30 pm, 6 pm). Avoid eating after 7 pm to allow 12 hours of fasting overnight, which supports MMC activity. Chew food thoroughly (20–30 minutes per meal).
key_levers.timeline: 2–4 weeks to establish routine; symptom improvement may follow.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Chronic stress suppresses stomach acid, impairs motility, and weakens gut immunity—all of which fuel SIBO. Sleep deprivation has similar effects. Addressing these factors reduces baseline bacterial load and speeds recovery.
key_levers.how_to_start: Start with 10 minutes of daily meditation or deep breathing. Aim for 10 pm bedtime with 7–9 hours sleep. Consider journaling, CBT, or therapy if stress is high. Progressive muscle relaxation can also help.
key_levers.timeline: 4–8 weeks to see GI benefits; sleep improvement often happens faster.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Antibiotics or herbal antimicrobials reduce bacterial load acutely, providing symptom relief and creating a window for dietary and lifestyle changes to take hold. However, treatment without addressing underlying causes leads to relapse in 20–30% of patients within months.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss with a gastroenterologist whether rifaxomicin, herbal antimicrobials, or other agents are appropriate. Coordinate treatment timing with dietary changes. Plan for post-treatment probiotics and dietary support.
key_levers.timeline: 2–4 weeks for antibiotic course; maximum benefit at 6–8 weeks with combined dietary support.

key_levers.lever: Build and maintain a healthy microbiome
key_levers.why_it_matters: A diverse, balanced microbiota competes with overgrown hydrogen-producing bacteria and supports intestinal barrier function and immunity. Rebuilding this after treatment is crucial for preventing relapse.
key_levers.how_to_start: After successful antimicrobial treatment, gradually reintroduce foods, especially prebiotic-rich foods (cooked asparagus, carrots, garlic if tolerated). Consider a targeted probiotic with well-studied strains (Lactobacillus plantarum, Bifidobacterium longum). Wait at least 2 weeks after antibiotic completion before starting probiotics.
key_levers.timeline: 8–12 weeks to see meaningful microbiota restoration; improvement in symptom tolerance often earlier.

key_levers.lever: Monitor and adjust based on symptoms and life changes
key_levers.why_it_matters: SIBO is often chronic or recurring; symptoms may fluctuate with stress, diet, illness, or travel. Regular check-ins with a clinician and willingness to adjust strategy prevent relapses and optimize quality of life.
key_levers.how_to_start: Schedule a 3–6 month follow-up appointment after initial treatment. Keep an ongoing symptom log. Note triggers (stress, travel, diet changes). Discuss whether repeat testing (breath test) is warranted.

common_mistakes: Treating SIBO with antibiotics alone without dietary change — bacteria return because substrate is still abundant. Combine antimicrobials with low-fermentation diet for best results.

Staying on a low-FODMAP diet indefinitely without reintroduction — this reduces fiber, restricts social eating, and deprives the microbiome of prebiotic foods needed for health. Use low-FODMAP as a temporary acute phase, not a lifetime diet.
Snacking or eating frequent small meals between main meals — this disrupts the migrating motor complex and feeds bacteria continuously. Eat three regular meals spaced 4–5 hours apart.

Assuming SIBO is cured after one course of antibiotics without repeat testing — relapse rates are 20–30% within 6 months if underlying motility or acid issues are not addressed. Confirm eradication with a breath test 4 weeks post-treatment.

Over-restricting diet based on food sensitivities without confirming SIBO diagnosis — many people restrict unnecessarily and worsen malnutrition or become socially isolated. Get a confirmed diagnosis first.

Ignoring sleep or stress despite dietary compliance — these factors are just as important as diet for healing. A stressed, sleep-deprived patient will not improve as much as one who addresses all three.

Continuing high-dose PPIs while treating SIBO — acid suppression is a major SIBO risk factor. Discuss with your doctor about tapering or stopping PPIs (if not medically essential) before or early in SIBO treatment.
Starting probiotics too early or without guidance — taking probiotics immediately after antibiotics can backfire; wait 2 weeks. Also, not all probiotics help SIBO; some strains may worsen symptoms.
Expecting rapid cure — SIBO typically improves over 8–12 weeks with combined treatment. Impatience often leads to premature switching of treatments or dietary non-adherence.

Not identifying the root cause (motility disorder, achlorhydria, post-infection state) — treating symptoms without addressing why SIBO developed leads to repeated relapses. Work with a specialist to investigate.

Relying on expensive functional medicine tests (stool analysis, organic acids) for diagnosis — breath testing or aspirate culture are more standardized and useful. Do not delay treatment waiting for alternative test results.

Consuming large amounts of 'SIBO-safe' products marketed online — replacements for bread, pasta, and sweets often use unusual ingredients (tapioca, coconut flour) that may still ferment. Whole foods are usually better.

specialists.role: Often the first to diagnose SIBO via breath testing or aspirate culture. Can assess for underlying motility disorders, IBS, or inflammatory bowel disease. Prescribes antibiotics like rifaxomicin. Best for confirming diagnosis and ruling out other GI conditions.
specialists.notes: Mainstream gastroenterologists may be skeptical of SIBO diagnosis or may rely only on breath testing without considering underlying causes. Some gastroenterologists have a special interest in SIBO and are well-versed in management.

specialists.type: Functional medicine doctor or integrative medicine practitioner
specialists.role: Often more willing to order breath testing and use antimicrobial protocols (antibiotics and herbal antimicrobials). Focuses on root-cause analysis, dietary management, and lifestyle optimization. May spend more time on patient education.
specialists.notes: Quality and expertise vary widely. Some overdiagnose SIBO and over-prescribe supplements. Verify credentials and ask about experience with SIBO specifically. Not all are evidence-based.

specialists.type: Registered Dietitian (preferably RD with FODMAP or SIBO specialization)
specialists.role: Guides diet management, including low-FODMAP elimination, reintroduction, and personalizing tolerances. Essential for long-term symptom management and preventing relapse. Can also manage nutrient deficiencies resulting from malabsorption.
specialists.notes: Finding a SIBO-knowledgeable dietitian is crucial. Ask for referrals from your gastroenterologist or check the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics website. Ensure they are an RD (registered), not an unregulated 'nutritionist'.

specialists.type: Naturopathic doctor (licensed ND in regulated states)
specialists.role: May prescribe herbal antimicrobials, probiotics, and lifestyle support. Useful for patients seeking alternatives to antibiotics or wanting support during herbal protocols.
specialists.notes: Licensing and expertise vary dramatically. Licensed NDs (in Washington, Oregon, etc.) are better regulated than unlicensed practitioners. Verify credentials and ask about SIBO-specific experience. Avoid practitioners who claim to 'cure' SIBO.

specialists.role: Can assess for risk factors (PPI use, medication side effects), refer to specialists, and provide supportive care. May be hesitant to diagnose or treat SIBO if unfamiliar with the condition.
specialists.notes: Educate your primary care doctor with current guidelines (e.g., from the American College of Gastroenterology). Advocate for referral to a gastroenterologist if your PCP is reluctant.

patient_communities.name: SIBO SOS (Small Intestinal Bacterial Overgrowth Support Online Community)
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community with patient-to-patient support, shared experiences, and resource sharing. Many members have been diagnosed and treated for SIBO. Good for practical advice but moderation is needed to avoid medical misinformation.

patient_communities.name: r/SIBO
patient_communities.notes: Forum for questions, symptom discussion, and treatment updates. Less formally moderated than Facebook; advice varies in quality. Useful for seeing real-world outcomes and dietary tips.

patient_communities.name: SIBO Doctor (Dr. Allison Siebecker's site and community)
patient_communities.notes: Comprehensive, evidence-based resource from a prominent SIBO researcher and clinician. Includes patient education, practitioner directory, and reliable information on diagnosis and treatment.

patient_communities.name: Canva SIBO support groups
patient_communities.notes: Some areas have local support groups or online communities specific to IBS and SIBO. Check Meetup.com or ask your healthcare provider for referrals.

patient_communities.name: FODMAPers (Low-FODMAP Diet Community)
patient_communities.notes: Useful for diet support, recipe sharing, and navigating low-FODMAP eating. Many SIBO patients also manage with low-FODMAP diets, so overlap is common.

learning_resources.title: SIBO: Tested Treatments for Optimal Gut Health
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive, evidence-based guide written by a leading SIBO researcher. Covers diagnosis, testing methods, antimicrobial and dietary treatments, and lifestyle management. Practical and balanced; accessible to patients and practitioners.

learning_resources.title: The FODMAP Solution: A Low FODMAP Diet Plan with Recipes and Meal Plans
learning_resources.description: Detailed guide to implementing a low-FODMAP diet. Includes shopping lists, meal plans, and recipes. Written by a registered dietitian. Essential resource for dietary management.

learning_resources.title: SIBO Information Resources
learning_resources.description: Free, comprehensive resource covering SIBO basics, testing, treatment options, and a directory of SIBO-knowledgeable practitioners. Regularly updated with evidence. One of the most reliable patient resources online.

learning_resources.title: The Low FODMAP Diet for IBS and Beyond
learning_resources.description: Official low-FODMAP resource from the institution that developed the diet. Includes certified low-FODMAP food list, mobile app, recipes, and research updates. Gold standard for dietary guidance.

learning_resources.title: Small Intestinal Bacterial Overgrowth (SIBO) - UpToDate
learning_resources.description: Evidence-based clinical summary covering SIBO diagnosis, pathophysiology, and management. Intended for healthcare providers but accessible to educated patients. Highly reliable and regularly updated.

learning_resources.title: The Microbiome Solution: A radical new way to heal your body from the inside out
learning_resources.description: Accessible overview of the gut microbiome and its role in health, including discussion of SIBO and dysbiosis. Written by a gastroenterologist; bridges patient and clinical perspectives.

learning_resources.title: Digestive Wellness: Strengthen the Immune System and Prevent Disease Through Healthy Digestion
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive book on gut health, including chapters on SIBO, dysbiosis, and dietary management. Evidence-based with practical strategies for long-term wellness.

learning_resources.title: SIBO Podcast (The SIBO Doctor Podcast)
learning_resources.description: Interview-based podcast covering SIBO diagnosis, treatment, and lifestyle management. Features SIBO experts, researchers, and patient stories. Episodes are free and archived on the SIBO Info website.

learning_resources.title: IBS-Dominant FODMAP: A new phenotype of IBS defined by associations between symptoms and dietary FODMAP levels
learning_resources.author_or_source: Journal of Clinical Gastroenterology and related peer-reviewed journals
learning_resources.description: Peer-reviewed research on SIBO, FODMAP sensitivity, and IBS overlap. For readers wanting to understand the science. Search PubMed for current studies.

learning_resources.title: Small Intestinal Bacterial Overgrowth: Prevalence, Diagnosis, and Therapeutic Approach
learning_resources.author_or_source: Clin Exp Gastroenterol, by R. Rezaie et al.
learning_resources.description: Review article synthesizing current evidence on SIBO prevalence, diagnostic methods, and treatment outcomes. Technical but comprehensive.

learning_resources.title: Breaking the Vicious Cycle: Intestinal Health Through Diet
learning_resources.description: Original description of the Specific Carbohydrate Diet (SCD), used by some SIBO patients. Older resource but still referenced. Useful if considering SCD as a dietary approach.

learning_resources.title: Gut and Psychology Syndrome
learning_resources.description: Explores connections between gut health and neurological function, including discussion of dysbiosis and SIBO. Personal narrative and case studies; less rigorous than some other resources but thought-provoking.

learning_resources.title: The Root Cause: How to Understand and Reverse Autoimmune, Thyroid, Metabolic and Digestive Conditions
learning_resources.description: Integrative approach to root-cause diagnosis of digestive and systemic conditions, including SIBO. Emphasizes personalized medicine and addresses motility, acid production, and microbiome collectively.

sections.relevance_signal: **Bloating that starts within 1–2 hours of eating**, especially carbohydrates. Symptoms even after clean low-FODMAP meals. Symptom relief with antibiotics in the past.
